lessonActivities.title: Unless – The Negative If! 🚫
lessonActivities.content:
Unless means 'if not' or 'except if'! 🔄 It's a concise way to express negative conditions.

Structure: Unless + positive clause (equivalent to If + negative clause)

  • Unless you hurry, you'll be late. (= If you don't hurry, you'll be late.)
  • Call me unless you're busy. (= Call me if you're not busy.)

Key point: Unless is followed by a positive verb form! 💡
examples: I won't go unless it stops raining.
Unless you study, you won't pass the exam.
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Unless Rule: Unless = if…not. Always use positive verb after unless. Do NOT use 'don't' after unless!

lessonActivities.title: As Long As / Provided That – With Conditions! 📋
lessonActivities.content:
As long as and provided that both mean 'if and only if' or 'on condition that'! ✅ They stress that the condition is essential.

As long as: Informal, common in spoken English

  • You can go out as long as you finish your homework.

Provided that: More formal, frequent in writing/contracts

  • The discount applies provided that you pay in cash.

Both require the condition to be fulfilled! 🎯
examples: I'll help you as long as you promise to try.
Entry is free provided that you register online.
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Rule: As long as = everyday English. Provided that = formal/official. Both = the condition MUST happen.

lessonActivities.title: In Case – For Precautions! 🛡️
lessonActivities.content:
In case means 'because something might happen' or 'to be prepared if…'! 🚨 It focuses on prevention/preparation.

Structure: Main clause + in case + present simple (even for future)

  • Take an umbrella in case it rains. (precaution for possible rain)
  • I'll leave early in case there's traffic.

NOT the same as 'if' — in case is about planning ahead! 🔮
examples: Buy extra food in case more people come.
Keep your phone charged in case of emergency.
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 In Case Rule: Use present simple after 'in case' even for future events. It answers 'Why are you doing this?' → preparation!

lessonActivities.title: Otherwise / Or Else – The Consequences! ⚠️
lessonActivities.content:
Otherwise and or else introduce the negative result if the advice/warning is ignored! 🚫

Otherwise: Neutral, common in both speaking & writing

  • Hurry up, otherwise we'll miss the train.

Or else: More informal/emotional, sometimes stronger tone

  • Be quiet, or else!

Both link an instruction to its bad consequence. 😱
examples: Wear a coat, otherwise you'll catch cold.
Finish your work on time, or else you'll be in trouble.
functions: Showing what happens if condition fails
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Rule: Otherwise/or else usually follow a comma after an imperative. They replace 'if not' in consequence sentences.

lessonActivities.title: Advanced Alternatives – Formal & Complex! 🎓
lessonActivities.content:
More sophisticated conditional connectors for academic, professional, or nuanced contexts! 📝

  • Supposing / Suppose: Hypothetical 'what if' questions
  • Supposing we miss the deadline, what then?
  • On condition that: Very formal = provided that
  • Access granted on condition that you sign the agreement.
  • Given that / Seeing that: Introducing known facts as the basis
  • Given that prices are rising, we should budget carefully.

Select according to formality and purpose! 🎭
examples: Suppose the flight is cancelled — how do we get home?
Seeing that you're experienced, we value your input.
functions: Introducing conditions based on known facts
lessonActivities.tips: 💡 Advanced Rule: Supposing = imagining. Given/Seeing that = because of known reality. On condition that = strict formal requirement.

writingActivities.question: Rewrite the following 6 sentences using 'unless' instead of 'if … not'.
writingActivities.hint: Example: If you don't leave now, you'll miss the train. → Unless you leave now, you'll miss the train.

writingActivities.question: Write 6 original sentences using 'unless' to give warnings or set exceptions.
writingActivities.hint: Example: You can't enter unless you have a ticket.

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences using 'as long as' and 3 using 'provided that' to express requirements or permissions.
writingActivities.hint: Example: You may leave early as long as you've finished your tasks.

writingActivities.question: Create 6 sentences using 'as long as' or 'provided that' in formal vs informal contexts (label which is which).
Write 7 sentences using 'in case' to describe sensible precautions in travel, work, or daily life.
writingActivities.hint: Example: I'm packing snacks in case we get hungry on the road.

writingActivities.question: Write 5 sentences using 'otherwise' and 3 using 'or else' to warn about consequences.
writingActivities.hint: Example: Turn off the lights, otherwise the bill will be high.

writingActivities.question: Write 6 sentences using 'supposing' or 'suppose' to discuss imaginary situations.
writingActivities.hint: Example: Supposing you could live anywhere, where would you choose?

writingActivities.question: Write 5 formal sentences using 'on condition that', 'given that', or 'seeing that'.
writingActivities.hint: Suitable for essays, emails to authorities, or reports.

writingActivities.question: Write a short story or dialogue (8–10 sentences) that naturally uses at least four different alternatives (unless, in case, otherwise, as long as, etc.).
writingActivities.hint: Example scenario: planning a trip with friends.

writingActivities.question: Write a paragraph (8–10 sentences) explaining your weekend plans using a mix of unless, in case, as long as, otherwise, and supposing.

gameActivities.question: Unless transformation:
gameActivities.sentence: If you don't study, you won't pass. → ____ you study, you won't pass.
gameActivities.hint: Unless replaces if + not → positive verb follows.

gameActivities.question: Choose the formal requirement:
gameActivities.sentence: Admission is allowed ____ you show ID.
gameActivities.hint: Provided that = formal version of as long as.

gameActivities.question: Precaution connector:
gameActivities.sentence: Bring a jacket ____ it gets cold later.
gameActivities.hint: In case = preparation for possible situation.

gameActivities.question: Consequence after imperative:
gameActivities.sentence: Don't touch that, ____ you'll get burned.
gameActivities.hint: Otherwise introduces bad result if ignored.

gameActivities.question: Hypothetical discussion starter:
gameActivities.sentence: ____ we win the lottery, what would you buy?

gameActivities.question: Common unless error – avoid double negative:
gameActivities.sentence: Unless you ____ hurry, we'll be late.

gameActivities.question: Informal vs formal requirement:
gameActivities.sentence: You can stay ____ you clean up afterwards. (casual)
gameActivities.hint: As long as = informal everyday use.

gameActivities.question: Fact-based connector:
gameActivities.sentence: ____ it's already 10 PM, we should go home.
gameActivities.hint: Given that / Seeing that = because of known fact.

gameActivities.question: Strong informal warning:
gameActivities.hint: Or else = stronger, more emotional tone.

gameActivities.question: Very formal condition:
gameActivities.sentence: Funds will be released ____ all documents are verified.
gameActivities.hint: On condition that = strictest formal version.

quizActivities.question: Which means the same as 'if … not'?
quizActivities.sentence: ____ you pay now, we cannot reserve the table.
quizActivities.explanation: Unless replaces 'if + negative clause' and uses a positive verb form.

quizActivities.question: 'Provided that' is best described as:
quizActivities.sentence: The contract is valid ____ both parties sign it.
quizActivities.hint: Provided that = formal equivalent of as long as.
quizActivities.explanation: Provided that is formal and common in legal/official language.

quizActivities.question: Which expresses preparation for a possible problem?
quizActivities.sentence: I'm taking cash ____ the card machine doesn't work.
quizActivities.hint: In case = precaution / just in case something happens.
quizActivities.explanation: In case is used when we do something to be ready for a possible future event.

quizActivities.question: Which shows the bad result if advice is ignored?
quizActivities.sentence: Put on sunscreen, ____ you'll get sunburned.
quizActivities.hint: Otherwise = what will happen if you don't follow the instruction.
quizActivities.explanation: Otherwise links an imperative to its negative consequence.

quizActivities.question: The most informal option among these is:
quizActivities.sentence: You can join us ____ you bring your own food.
quizActivities.explanation: As long as is the everyday, informal version of 'if and only if'.

quizActivities.question: Common mistake with 'unless':
options: Unless you don't hurry, we'll be late.
Unless you hurry, we'll be late.
Unless you will hurry, we'll be late.
quizActivities.correctAnswer: Unless you hurry, we'll be late.
quizActivities.explanation: Unless is followed by positive present simple — never add 'don't'.

quizActivities.question: Which if alternative should you choose?
quizActivities.sentence: I'll text you ____ I get lost.
quizActivities.explanation: In case + present simple even when referring to future — common error is adding 'will'.

quizActivities.question: Which is used for 'what if' imaginary scenarios?
quizActivities.sentence: ____ it snows tomorrow — shall we still meet?
quizActivities.explanation: Suppose is used to discuss imaginary or hypothetical situations.

quizActivities.question: 'Given that' and 'seeing that' introduce:
quizActivities.sentence: ____ fuel prices are high, public transport is better.
quizActivities.hint: Given that = because we already know this is true.
quizActivities.explanation: Given that / Seeing that present a real, existing situation as the basis.

quizActivities.question: Which is strongest/most threatening?
quizActivities.hint: Or else can sound like a threat in informal speech.
quizActivities.explanation: Or else is more informal and can carry a stronger/emotional tone than otherwise.

spellingActivities.hint: Something that must be done or had
Action taken to prevent danger or problems
spellingActivities.sentence: Take medicine as a ____ in case you feel sick.

spellingActivities.hint: Result or effect of an action

spellingActivities.sentence: In case prepares for a future ____.

spellingActivities.hint: Something not included in a general rule
spellingActivities.sentence: Unless makes an ____ to the rule.

spellingActivities.hint: A situation that must exist before something else happens
spellingActivities.sentence: As long as sets an important ____.
Provided that and on condition that are very ____.
